coredata.dc:title: Magnetofluorescent photothermal micelles packaged with GdN@CQDs as photothermal and chemical dual-modal therapeutic agents
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ming
Wang, Jing
Wang, Wentao
Zhang, Jun
Zhou, Ninglin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots could be extended in biological applications for drug delivery and photothermal therapeutics due to their ability to improving therapeutic effect and reducing side effects. Here, we use doxorubicin (DOX) as the model drug, the amino and carboxyl coated magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots (GdN@CQDs) as a carrier, Genipin (GP) as cross-linking agent to design and prepare a multifunctional drug delivery system triggered by near-infrared (NIR) light and pH dual-stimuli. The cytotoxicity of the nanoparticles has been examined by MTT assay. The fluorescent GdN@CQDs/GP serves as both a controllable drug release gatekeeper and a fluorescent probe for the visualization of the drug delivery process. In vitro and in vivo experiments showed that this platform can deliver anti-cancer drugs to cells, releasing them intracellular upon NIR irradiation, and effectively eliminate tumors through chemo-photothermal synergistic therapeutic effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Near-infrared
Drug delivery system
Chemo-photothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amino and carboxyl coated GdN@CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration for the formation of amino and carboxyl coated GdN@CQDs, GdN@CQDs/GP, and DOX loading. (B) HRTEM (inset) and TEM microimages of GdN@CQDs. (C) TEM microimages of GdN@CQDs/GP. (D) HRTEM (inset) and TEM microimages of the individual GdN@CQDs.

Characterization of GdN@CQDs and GdN@CQDs/GP. (A) XPS of the GdN@CQDs and N@CQDs. (B) C1s XPS spectra of GdN@CQDs. (C) FTIR spectra of GdN@CQDs, GP, and GdN@CQDs/GP. (D) 13C NMR of the GdN@CQDs (a), GP (b), and GdN@CQDs/GP (c).

(A) Upconversion PL spectra of GdN@CQDs/GP obtained with different excitation wavelength. (B) CLSM images of A549 cells incubated with GdN@CQDs/GP for 8h observed under 403nm (a), 488nm (b), 543nm (c), and their merged images (d), respectively. Scale bar=30µm. (C) The r1 relaxivity curve of GdN@CQDs. Inset: in vitro MR images of GdN@CQDs prepared using a series of Gd concentrations: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0mM. The concentration of Gd3+ was quantified using an inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, JY 2000–2, Jobin Yvon Horiba). (D) The absorption of water-dispersible GdN@CQDs/GP (50µg/mL). The insets show enlarged absorption of the GdN@CQDs/GP from 550 to 750nm.

(A) Temperature elevation of pure water and the aqueous dispersion of GdN@CQDs/GP with different concentrations under irradiation from a 671nm laser with a power of 2.0W/cm2 as a function of irradiation time (0–480s). (B) UV-visible absorption of the GdN@CQDs, DOX, GP, GdN@CQDs/GP and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX. Inset magnifies the absorption in the range between 380 and 580nm. (C) LSCM images of HeLa cells incubated with GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX for 5h observed under bright field (a), 488nm (b), 543nm (c), and their merged images (d), respectively. Scale bar=30µm.

(A) A diagram illustrating stimuli-responsive drug release from GdN@CQDs-encapsulated GP nanospheres. (B) The release profile of DOX from GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX. (C) Cytotoxicity assay of HeLa cells after 48h of incubation with GdN@CQDs/GP, DOX, GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX (671nm laser, 2.0W/cm2, and 8min). Error bars represent standard deviations from five repeated experiments.

IR thermal imaging of intratumoral PBS, GdN@CQDs/GP, and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX injected mice tumor sites at 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8min after irradiation by 671nm laser at 2.0W/cm2. (B) Change in temperature at the mice tumor sites as a function of irradiation time. (C) In vivo red fluorescence images obtained after intravenous injections of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX in nude mice via the tail vein. From left to right: bright field (a), and overlay of bright field and fluorescence (b). (D) In vitro fluorescence images of major organs and tumor dissected from mice injected with GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX at 24h. (E) Biodistribution of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX in major organs excised from mice 24h post intravenous injection (n=5).

(A) Representative photographs of the tumor-bearing mice after various treatments on the first and 20days. Time-dependent mouse relative weight curves after different treatments. (C) Time-dependent relative tumor growth curves after different treatments. (D) Representative photographs of the tumors collected from the mice after various treatments. (E) Histological images observed from heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor tissue stained with H&E to detect tissue damage in response to PBS, DOX GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX-DOX, GdN@CQDs/GP +671nm laser, and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX +671nm laser. Error bars represent standard deviations from five repeated experiments.

head.ce:title: Magnetofluorescent photothermal micelles packaged with GdN@CQDs as photothermal and chemical dual-modal therapeutic agents

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots could be extended in biological applications for drug delivery and photothermal therapeutics due to their ability to improving therapeutic effect and reducing side effects. Here, we use doxorubicin (DOX) as the model drug, the amino and carboxyl coated magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots (GdN@CQDs) as a carrier, Genipin (GP) as cross-linking agent to design and prepare a multifunctional drug delivery system triggered by near-infrared (NIR) light and pH dual-stimuli. The cytotoxicity of the nanoparticles has been examined by MTT assay. The fluorescent GdN@CQDs/GP serves as both a controllable drug release gatekeeper and a fluorescent probe for the visualization of the drug delivery process. In vitro and in vivo experiments showed that this platform can deliver anti-cancer drugs to cells, releasing them intracellular upon NIR irradiation, and effectively eliminate tumors through chemo-photothermal synergistic therapeutic effect.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel delivery system, the Drug Delivery Systems (DDSs) based on nanoparticles have many advantages, such as stimulating response, targeting therapy, imaging and tracking drugs [1–6]. Therefore, this type of DDSs has attracted great attentions in anti-cancer field. Nowadays, magnetic fluorescent quantum dots (QDs), with the combined advantages from magnetic and fluorescent materials, can achieve a variety of functions such as the magnetic separation, targeted identification, fluorescence imaging and magnetic resonance imaging simultaneously [7–8]. Therefore, several research groups have recently devoted to the development of heavy metal QDs. Magnetic fluorescent QDs with potential therapeutic applications have received high attention in numerous fields, while these QDs are intrinsically cytotoxic as they release free radicals of heavy metals from the QDs core into the blood stream [9]. The ideal magnetic fluorescent nanoparticles should have high relaxivity, strong fluorescence, targeted drug delivery, high stability, low toxicity, and excellent compatibility with biological systems. For example, Gd doped magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots are considered to be advantageous over other magnetic metal QDs with respect to biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost [10,11].

Photothermal therapy, a minimally invasive therapeutic methodology, employs hyperthermia generated by photothermal agents from laser energy to kill cancer cells [12,13]. In order to improve the therapeutic effects, it is necessary to explore high efficient photothermal conversion nanomaterials [14,15]. Meanwhile, further development of multifunctional nanocomposites is beneficial to rote various technologies together for comprehensive treatments of cancer. Consequently, multifunctional nanocomposites could deliver anti-cancer drugs to cells, release them intracellularly upon NIR irradiation, and effectively reduce tumor cells through chemo-photothermal synergistic therapeutics. Recently, increasing attention is being drawn toward magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots due to their good biocompatibility and strong NIR absorbility [16]. Indeed, intrinsic theranostic applications of magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots have become the key point.

Doxorubicin (DOX), a commonly used chemotherapeutic drug, has broad spectrum of anti-tumor effect due to its effective inhibition of the RNA and DNA synthesis [17,18]. However, its toxic and side effect is very severe, such as the cause of the decrease of white blood cell and platelet, hair loss, cardiac toxicity, arrhythmia and bone marrow suppression. Hence, its clinical application is restricted [19–21]. The best way to attenunate the side effects of drugs is to reduce drug intake by normal tissues or organs. In view of this, it makes sense to develop a new drug delivery system with high efficiency, controlled release and multi functions, which will be helpful to reduce the side effects of drugs [22,23]. Thus, the magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots can be used to reduce the limitations of DOX [24–26]. Simultaneously, the stimuli for the controlled release could be: pH, magnetic field, electric field, ultrasound, NIR light, enzymatic hydrolysis, etc. Because the pH of tumor tissues is lower than that of normal tissues, the pH stimulus has great application potential [27,28]. Moreover, carbon nanomaterials (including MFCQD, CDs, and CNT) show strong NIR absorption and high photothermal conversion efficiency. The release of drug could be selectively stimulated by NIR-light, enabling intracellular drug accumulation and thereby enhancing the efficiency to kill cancer cell [29]. Here, low pH value and NIR are considered as the ideal trigger,which can achieve the selective release and targeted transport of anticancer drugs. Taking the advantages of pH and NIR as dual triggers, the pH and NIR-targeted drug releases have been designed and explored to effectively trigger drug release in tumor cells [30–32].

In this study, we chose DOX as a drug model, the amino and carboxyl coated magnetofluorescent GdN@CQDs as a drug carrier, genipin as the cross-linking agent, designed and prepared a multifunctional drug delivery system with pH-triggered drug release. As a water-soluble bi-functional crosslinking reagent, genipin is not cytotoxic for the animal and human cells so far examined [33,34]. Therefore, we crosslinked genipin to GdN@CQDs, and then loaded DOX in the carrier (GdN@CQDs/GP) by covalent bond and electrostatic interaction. Moreover, the biocompatibility of GdN@CQDs embedded inside polymeric spheres may be better than that of the GdN@CQDs-coated polymeric spheres, whereas GdN@CQDs is protected by the polymeric spheres in the former system. The structure of all the intermediates and final products was characterized and confirmed by 13C NMR, UV and FT-TR. In the drug loading system, carbon dots have good water solubility, uniform particle distribution, low cell toxicity and great biocompatibility. GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX is sensitive to pH and NIR, so the pH and NIR dual stimuli have great application potential, which would control the release of anticancer drugs. The GdN@CQDs/GP demonstrates strong NIR absorption and high photothermal conversion efficiency. In addition, the drug-loaded nanospheres exhibit chemo-photothermal synergistic therapeutic effect upon laser irradiation, which has apparent potential for cancer phototherapy.

Citrate acid and L-Lysine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Genipin (GP) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Doxorubicin (DOX) and GdCl3·6H2O was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Chloroacetic acid was purchased from Shanghai Jinshan Chemical Plant (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Double-distilled water (18.2MΩ) was obtained from Milli-Q integral system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Mouse embryonic fibroblast cell (NIH3T3), human cervical cancer cell (HeLa), and human pulmonary adenocarcinoma cell (A549) were provided by the Drum Tower Hospital of Jiangsu Province. Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Thermo Fisher HyClone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was from SunShine Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) was purchased from Amresco (USA). Hoechst 33258 was from Gibco Co., Ltd. (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino and carboxyl coated GdN@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Citrate acid (1.2g), GdCl3·6H2O (0.3g), and L-Lysine (0.5g) were dissolved in 20mL deionized water. After scattering for 30min, the mixture was sealed into a 50mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 5h. After cooling to room temperature, a yellow solution was obtained and named GdN@CQDs. To further purify the GdN@CQDs, the solution was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min to remove small volumes of liquid and filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove large or agglomerated particles. The purification of the GdN@CQDs was conducted through a dialysis tube (3500Da molecular weight cut off) for about 24h in dark. The obtained GdN@CQDs solution was dried to a powder by vacuum freeze-drying machine and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C prior to use [35].

GdN@CQDs (40 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL deionized water to form a homogeneous solution and adjusted to pH=8.0. Then aqueous genipin (0.5wt%) was slowly added to the GdN@CQDs solution for crosslinking reaction at 60°C for 5h to obtain GdN@CQDs/GP. The resulting solution was blue color and filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove large particles. A powder was obtained by vacuum freeze-drying machine. Finally, the powder was rinsed twice with ethyl acetate then freeze-dried and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C prior to use [36].

The photoluminescent (PL) spectra were recorded using molecular fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, varian, USA). (FT-IR) Nexus 670 FTIR type (Nicolet) infrared spectrometer was used to analyze the infrared spectrum of the sample. The fluorescence was recorded using the Time-resolved Fluorescence Life Time Microscope (FM-4P-TCSPC, Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D/Max 2500V/PC diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). The thermogravimetric measurements were performed on a Perkin-Elmer TG 7 instrument using a heating rate of 10°C/min up to 800°C in nitrogen atmosphere. Ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using UV absorption spectrophotometer (Cary-50, varian, USA). Proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR and 13C NMR) was measured by 400MHz WB Solid-State NMR Spectrometer (Switzerland). The surface composition and element analysis of the samples were recorded using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab-250, Thermo, USA). The Malvern ZEN 3600 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK) was used to study the hydrodynamic size and Zeta potential. The morphologies of the samples were characterized using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi, Japan) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-5610LV, Japan). MR images were acquired by a 7T BioSpec 70/30 experimental scanner (70/30 Bruker BioSpin; Ettlingen, Germany). The fluorescent images of cells were acquired by confocal laser scanning microscope (TI-E-A1R, Nikon, Japan) and upright fluorescent microscope (XSP-63X, Nikon, Japan).

Firstly, 0.1mol/L PBS buffer solution with pH 7 was prepared by Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 to mimick the physiological conditions, and the acidic buffer solution with pH 6.5 and 5 was prepared with acetic acid salt to simulate the acidic conditions. The GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX (5.0mg) and GdN@CQDs/DOX (5.0mg) were dissolved in 5mL PBS and scattered for 30min. The mixture was placed in the dialysis bag (MWCO 500–1000Da) and dialyzed in 95mL PBS with the stirring rate and temperature of 100r/min and 37°C, respectively. Two milliliters of PBS was removed at different timepoints and same volume of fresh PBS was added. Finally, the ultraviolet–visible spectra were measured for samples taken at different timepoints at 485nm and the DOX release was calculated [37].
At designed time intervals, the samples were irradiated by the 671nm laser with a power intensity of 2.0W/cm2 for 8min.

Female nude mice (20g) were injected subcutaneously with the cell suspension containing 1×106 HeLa cells. When the tumor size reached approximately 100–120mm3, the mice were randomly divided into five groups (5 mice per group): saline-treatment only; DOX-treatment only; GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX-treatment only; GdN@CQDs/GP-treatment and irradiation; and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX-treatment and irradiation. The tumor sizes were measured every other day after the treatment. Tumor volume (V) was determined by the following equation:
V =
ab
2
/ 2
where a and b are the length and width of the tumor, respectively. The relative tumor volume was normalized to its initial size before treatment and laser irradiation. After 20days, the mice were killed and the tumor and major organs (heart, lung, liver, spleen, kidney, and tumor) were collected, fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin, embedded in paraffin, cut into 4μm sections, and stained using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Finally, the images of these histological tissue sections were taken under an optical microscope.

To the as-obtained amino and carboxyl coated GdN@CQDs, we selected Gd, N co-doped CQDs prepared through citrate acid, GdCl3·6H2O and L-Lysine for detailed study. In the formation process of GdN@CQDs (Fig. 1 A), Gd3+ was captured by carboxyl or amino groups on the citrate acid and L-Lysine by coordination. Later, citrate acid and L-Lysine would occur dehydration and condensation polymerization through amidation reaction [38]. Then the resulting peptide chains were carbonized with the extended reaction time. Finally, N co-doped carbon clusters were grown into bigger amino and carboxyl coated GdN@CQDs.

In this study, GP, a natural and non-toxic crosslinking reagent was used to cross-link GdN@CQDs. Chemotherapy, using GdN@CQDs combined with photothermal therapy, appears to be a more aggressive approach compared to other individual methods of treatment. The main formation methods of the GdN@CQDs-coated polymer spheres are as follows: a highly efficient crosslinking reagent was used to incorporate GdN@CQDs into GP nanospheres; drugs were adsorbed onto GP-modified GdN@CQDs by charge-charge interaction. It was observed that the color of the genipin crosslinked GdN@CQDs nanospheres turned dark-bluish (Fig. S1). The color was deepened with the increase of crosslinking time. Bluish color attributed to the double bonds in the GP cross-linking molecules (Fig. S1).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the GdN@CQDs and GdN@CQDs/GP are well dispersed and uniform with an diameter of ca 3.8 and 102nm, respectively (Fig. 1B and C). The high-resolution HRTEM images provided in the insets showed that all of the GdN@CQDs exhibited identical well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene. Interestingly, there are many dark particles wrapped in the nanosphere as shown in the TEM image of single GdN@CQDs/GP (Fig. 1C and D), suggesting that GdN@CQDs are wrapped after chemical crosslinking reaction. This value is also consistent with the size determined from dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (average diameter=6.7±0.6 and 88±15nm) (Fig. S2A, a and b). However, it is slightly different from that measured by TEM. This is mainly due to the different method for sample preparation. Comparatively, several ellipsoidal particles of about 4nm in size are also present on the surface of the nanosphere (Fig. 1D). We found that the increase in particle size of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX compared to free GdN@CQDs/GP indicated that GdN@CQDs/GP were decorated by DOX conjugates (Fig. S2A, c). This size of the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX is well within the preferred range of the nanoparticles used for drug delivery. The functional performance of nanoparticle-based drug carrier depends on the physicochemical properties such as size and charge state. Mean particle sizes of GdN@CQDs/GP were 25±8, 42±12, 50±15, 78±15, and 146±20nm, respectively, for particles after reacting for 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5h. Mean zeta potential of GdN@CQDs/GP was 8.2, 3.6, −6.5, −12.3 and −14.6mV, respectively (Fig. S3), which indicated that the nanospheres was coated with more GdN@CQDs as increasing the reaction time from 1 to 5h. Hence, the reaction time of 4h was selected for the following experiments. For biomedical applications, good dispersion of nanomaterials in a physiological medium is a prime merit. As shown in Fig. S2B, the solubilization and fluorescence of GdN@CQDs/GP exhibited excellent stability in all solutions for more than one month.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, GdN@CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen with the binding energy peaks at 142.8, 1187.2, 284.2, 402.56, and 532.44eV corresponding to Gd 4d, Gd 3d, O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively. Elemental analysis in Fig. 2A showed the composition of GdN@CQDs as C 62.51, N 11.68, O 18.55, and Gd 7.25wt%. No Cl ions were detected by XPS analysis, indicating that unreacted GdCl3 was removed completely. The high-resolution XPS 4d spectra of GdN@CQDs (Fig. S4A) exhibited peaks at 142.8 and 147.8eV. Again, GdN@CQDs exhibited Gd 3d5/2 line at 1187.2eV (Fig. S4B), strongly suggesting the successful chelation of Gd3+ onto the surface of CQDs. No such peaks (Gd 4d at 142.8eV and Gd 3d at 1187.2eV) could be detected for the N@CQDs, strongly suggesting the successful chelation of Gd3+ onto the surface of N@CQDs. Meanwhile, the C 1s spectrum of GdN@CQDs demonstrates four peaks at 283.9, 284.8, 287.1, and 287.8eV (Fig. 2B), which are associated with C 1s states in CC, CC, CO, and CN, respectively. The O 1s spectrum of the GdN@CQDs in Fig. S4C is deconvoluted into four peaks at 530.2, 531.8, 532.5 and 532.8eV, which are attributed to OCO, OCO, CO, and OC-O, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4D, the high resolution spectra of N 1s shows the pyridinic-like N (399.1eV), pyrrolic-like N (399.9eV), NH (400.6eV), NO (401.4eV), and N-(C)3 (397.6eV), respectively. The N 1s peak of GdN@CQDs at 399.1eV was assigned to -NH2 and N at the surface [39].

The vibration band at 1680cm−1 is attributed to the carbonyl (CO) groups, and a broadband at 3420cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of amine and hydroxyl groups, which indicate the existence of amino and carboxyl of the GdN@CQDs. For GdN@CQDs/GP, the significantly arisen adsorption at 1625cm−1 and disappeared adsorption at 1580cm−1 could be attributed to the formation of secondary amide and the absorption of NH2 as a result of the reaction between amino groups on GdN@CQDs and ester groups of GP (Fig. 2C). The crosslinking of GdN@CQDs and GP mechanism was further examined by 13C NMR (Fig. 2D). In the frequency range 150–160ppm, the resonance attributed to C-3 of GP was decreased after the crosslinking of GdN@CQDs by GP [33], indicating a nucleophilic attack by NH2 of GdN@CQDs on the olefinic carbon atom at C-3 of GP, followed by opening of the dihydropyran ring. The resonance appearing at 180ppm could be attributed to the formation of amide through the reaction of the amino group on GdN@CQDs with the ester group of GP, and we found the characteristic peak of GdN@CQDs and GP. Meanwhile, the active hydroxyl group of GP easily forms an ester bonded with the carboxyl group of GdN@CQDs.

The excitation and emission of GdN@CQDs are located at 360 and 455nm (Fig. S5A). The photoluminescence emission spectra (Fig. S5B) depended on the excitation wavelength (the fluorescent peak red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength from 290nm to 420nm). As shown in Fig. S5C, we found that the excitation and emission of GdN@CQDs/GP were located at 345 and 430nm, respectively. Therefore, we selected 345nm as the excitation wavelength for the following experiments. Furthermore, similar to GdN@CQDs, the prepared GdN@CQDs/GP also exhibited excitation-dependent emission characteristics (Fig. S5D). The PL spectra of the GdN@CQDs/GP excited by long-wavelength light from 1000 to 700nm (Fig. 3 A) clearly demonstrate the upconverted emissions from 550 to 445nm. The upconversion property of the GdN@CQDs/GP can be attributed to the multiphoton active process similar to the previously reported CDs [40]. Importantly, the GdN@CQDs/GP can emit light in the NIR range under the excitation of NIR light, which is highly desirable for biological applications. Simultaneously, the λex-dependent PL behavior was useful for multicolor in vivo biological imaging applications (Fig. 3B). The inset of Fig. 3A and Fig. S5C showed the photograph of GdN@CQDs and GdN@CQDs/GP under visible light and under a UV lamp (365nm), and we found that the color of GdN@CQDs/GP had changed. The lifetime of the GdN@CQDs and GdN@CQDs/GP was 4.17 and 3.22ns. (Fig. S6A). Simultaneously, almost no bleaching was observed after continuous irradiation with UV light (365nm) for 2h (Fig. S6B), suggesting the emission was relative stable. The GdN@CQDs/GP exhibited excellent stability even the NaCl concentration reached 1mol/L (Fig. S6C), indicating that the prepared GdN@CQDs/GP could work at higher salt concentration. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of the GdN@CQDs and GdN@CQDs/GP was calculated to be 6.8% and 6.2%, respectively.

Fig. 3C showed the gray-scaled T1-weighted MR images, and the inset presented the linear plot of relaxation rates versus equivalent Gd3+ concentration. As illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3C, concentration-dependent positive contrast enhancement was observed for dispersions, validating their function of curtailing the longitudinal relaxation time of hydrogen protons. The r1 relaxivity curves showed an apparent enhancement of relaxation with the concentration of Gd3+ increased. The r1 relaxivity of GdN@CQDs/GP was 6.27mM−1 s−1, higher than that from commercially available MRI agent (Gd-DTPA) under the same tested conditions [8]. GdN@CQDs/GP containing minimum amount of Gd content while exhibiting a higher longitudinal relaxivity are still highly desirable. These results suggested that GdN@CQDs/GP significantly magnify the longitudinal proton relaxation process.

The thermostability of GdN@CQDs and GdN@CQDs/GP was studied by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), as shown in Fig. S7A. Compared with GdN@CQDs, GdN@CQDs/GP exhibited better thermal stability up to 800 °C with releasing of pyrogas (CO2, NO, CO, and NO2), and carbon materials with a yield of almost 62% [31]. Meanwhile, the TGA measurement of GdN@CQDs/GP indicated that GdN@CQDs had been successfully grafted onto the GP. The zeta potentials of GdN@CQDs, GdN@CQDs/GP, DOX, and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX were −7.2, −12.3, 12.8, and 1.4mV (Fig. S6), suggested that the introduction of GP increased the negative charge of GdN@CQDs. DOX can thus be loaded onto GdN@CQDs/GP by the electrostatic interactions between DOX and GdN@CQDs/GP [38].

Fig. 3D showed the UV-vis spectrum of GdN@CQDs/GP, with a strong Soret absorption at 300nm, and weak Q-bands between 500 and 650nm. Their absorption in the NIR region from 650 to 750nm made the as-prepared GdN@CQDs/GP highly promising for photothermal therapy. Given their efficient NIR absorption features between 650 and 800nm, the GdN@CQDs/GP was investigated for their potential use in photothermal ablation therapy of cancer using a 671nm laser. Under continuous irradiation of 671nm laser (2.0W/cm−2), the temperature elevation of the aqueous dispersions containing GdN@CQDs/GP at different concentrations (0–100µg/mL) was measured (Fig. 4 A). The control experiment demonstrated that the temperature of pure water (without GdN@CQDs/GP) only increased by less than 6.4°C. However, with the addition of GdN@CQDs/GP (0.02, 0.05, 0.08, and 0.1mg/mL), the temperature of the aqueous dispersions increased by 12.2–23.5°C after an 8min irradiation. Thus, the photothermal conversion capacity of GdN@CQDs/GP was chosen and further investigated in the following experiments (Fig. S7B). At the concentration (0.1mg/mL) of GdN@CQDs/GP, the temperature increased up to 50.2°C after an 8min irradiation, while that of the pure water slightly changed, confirming the fast and high-efficient photothermal conversion capacity of GdN@CQDs/GP.

We speculated that DOX was probably conjugated with GdN@CQDs/GP through physicochemical interactions, including (π-π) stacking, electrostatic, and hydrophobic interactions [9] as shown in Fig. 1A. The UV-visible absorption was showed in Fig. 4B. Specifically, compared with free GP, GdN@CQDs/GP displayed a sharp absorption peak centered at 230nm originating from the GdN@CQDs, which indicated that GdN@CQDs was crosslinked with GP successfully. Meanwhile, crosslinking was observed as the color changed from transparent to blue color after treatment with GP. GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 485nm and a sharp absorption peak at 300nm resulting from the GdN@CQDs, compared with free DOX. The surface zeta potential of GdN@CQDs was changed from −12.6±3.51 to 2.3±3.23mV after DOX load (Fig. S8). It suggested that the negative charge (COO−) of the GdN@CQDs/GP conjugated with a positive charge (NH2) on DOX, which may be due to electrostatic interaction. The attachment of DOX onto the surface of GdN@CQDs/GP nanospheres was also validated by 1H NMR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. S9A, a and b, the chemical shift at δ 7.5 is the proton vibration peak on the phenyl ring of DOX. As shown in Fig. S9B, the fluorescence of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX contained the same excitation peaks of GdN@CQDs and DOX, which confirmed that the final product contained the GdN@CQDs and DOX. The fluorescence of the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX displays the λex-dependent property of the GdN@CQDs. Fig. 1A showed the schematic representation of green fabrication of GdN@CQDs/GP and DOX loading process. The GdN@CQDs, GdN@CQDs/GP, and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX solution displayed green, blue, pink luminescence under UV irradiation. Additionally, compared with GdN@CQDs/DOX (13.1%), the DOX loading rate of GdN@CQDs/GP was calculated as high as 31.4%. The GdN@CQDs with GP could provide efficient loading of DOX, because DOX could load onto the surface of GdN@CQDs/GP nanospheres.

The HeLa cells were used for live cell imaging. The cellular uptake of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX and intracellular drug release were investigated by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). As shown in Fig. 3B, the blue, green, and red fluorescence of cell with GdN@CQDs/GP was observed under an excitation wavelengths of 403, 488, and 543nm, respectively. Interestingly, the GdN@CQDs/GP fluorescence in the merged image was also found mainly in the cell cytoplasm, indicating that GdN@CQDs/GP was an ideal drug delivery vehicle. Meanwhile, the cells nuclear emitted red fluorescence under an excitation wavelength of 543nm (Fig. 4C), indicating that DOX was released from the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX and penetrated into the cell nuclei. The nuclear regions of the HeLa cells exhibited strong red emissions, indicating that DOX was almost entirely released from GdN@CQDs/GP and could diffuse to the cell nuclei, which is in complete agreement with free DOX intracellular imaging shown in Fig. S10 and other reports [41]. Furthermore, fluorescence intensity across the image measured by Image J was uniform for GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX. The cellular internalization and intracellular drug release behaviors of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX were schematically illustrated in Fig. 5 A, DOX and GdN@CQDs/GP was released and then DOX entered and assembled in the nuclei under the acidic environment of lysosomes.

Releasing of DOX from the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX was studied by dialyzing in PBS [32]. The DOX release from the GdN@CQDs/GP was performed at pH 5.0, 6.0, and 7.4 (the body environment of tumor cells and normal cells). As shown in Fig. 5B, the release profiles of DOX depended on the pH and NIR. At pH=7.4, the release of DOX increased slowly, reaching a maximum value of 7%. On the other hand, at pH 6.0, about 45% of DOX was released after 60 h. However, at pH 5.0 buffer, we observed that the release of DOX nearly reached 61%, showing that DOX could be released at a much faster and higher rate from GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX in acidic condition. The amount of cumulative released DOX at pH 5.0 buffer was enhanced by NIR-light irradiation. The increased drug release upon NIR-light irradiation could be attributed to the increased temperature due to the effective photothermal heating property of GdN@CQDs/GP, which might result in the destruction of intermolecular forces and hydrogen bonding. Therefore, the DOX release was enhanced. Consequently, GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX could release the DOX molecule under exogenous NIR irradiation and the lower pH in endolysosome, which would enhance anti-tumor efficacy and minimize the side effect of the drug.

The concentration-dependent toxicity of GdN@CQDs/GP, DOX and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX was performed in two types of cancer cells (HeLa, A549 cells) and one normal cells (NIH3T3 cells). We founded that GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX preferred to kill cancer cells while protecting normal cells. The viability of HeLa cells (Fig. 5C) and A549 cells (Fig. S11A) was reduced with different doses of GdN@CQDs/GP, DOX and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX for 48h, respectively. The livability of HeLa and A549 cells was reduced to nearly 30–40%. Moreover, GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX caused a significant increase in cell death at all concentrations in HeLa cells and A549 cells. Interestingly, at a similar dosage, GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX caused a significant increase in cell death at a much lower rate than free DOX for the NIH3T3 cells (Fig. S11B), indicating that GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX exhibited more inhibited effect on cancer cells as compared to normal cells. Meanwhile, we also performed MTT measurements to confirm the photothermal therapeutic effects of the GdN@CQDs/GP nanocomposites on the HeLa cells and A549 cells. The results showed that the viability of cells incubated with the nanocomposites decreased rapidly with the increase of the irradiation time. In addition, we investigated the effect of chemophotothermal therapy by the MTT assay. We founded that the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX with NIR irradiation exhibited the highest rate of the death cancer cells compared to DOX (chemotherapy), treatment of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX without NIR irradiation (chemotherapy). It was worthy to be noted that the nanoparticles improve the therapy efficiency compared with individual photothermal or chemotherapy.

To verify the in vivo photothermal conversion effect generated by the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX, we used an IR thermal mapping apparatus to record the change in temperature in the tumor area (Fig. 6 A). As expected, the temperature of tumor site treated with GdN@CQDs/GP and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX was rapidly increased and maintained at 50.2°C during laser irradiation (Fig. 6A). This temperature was sufficient to cause irreversible damage to cancer cells [42,43]. In comparison, the maximum temperature on the tumor surface in the laser-only group was approximately 30.8°C under the same irradiation condition, which demonstrated that the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX played a key role in heat generation.

To confirm that GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX could achieve tumor-specific accumulation in vivo, a model of HeLa cell-bearing mice was established. As shown in Fig. 6C, fluorescence signal was clearly detected in tumor sites for the mice receiving intravenous injection. After 24h injection, mice were killed and their major organs (i.e., heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidneys) and tumors were excised and imaged to evaluate fluorescence intensities. The excised tumor tissue showed strong fluorescence intensity, whereas the spleen and heart showed very low signals. The excised kidney tissue showed stronger fluorescence intensity than the heart and spleen. The GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX mainly accumulated in the liver, lung, and kidney tissues (Fig. 6D). GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX accumulation in the tumor was further quantified by identifying the tumor as a region of interest (ROI) to measure its FL intensity (Fig. 6E).

We proceeded to estimate the synergistic anti-tumor efficiency of GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX on tumor-bearing mice. As shown in Fig. 7 , female nude mice bearing HeLa tumors were divided into five groups with 6 mice per group, PBS (Group 1), DOX (Group 2), GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX-DOX (Group 3), GdN@CQDs/GP +671nm laser (Group 4), and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX +671nm laser (Group 5). The real photographs of tumor-bearing nude mice were recorded in different groups, respectively (Fig. 7A). The tumor sizes and body weights were then measured every other day for 20 days (Fig. 7B and C). At 20days, three mice were sacrificed and tumors were excised. The sizes of tumors in the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX +671nm laser group were obviously smaller than those in the control group (Fig. 7D). Although enhanced inhibition of tumor growth was observed with injection of only DOX, GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX, and GdN@CQDs/GP with NIR laser irradiation, the tumor growth was only partially delayed, indicating the poor efficacy in inhibiting tumor growth. Compared to treatment with DOX and GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX without laser irradiation showed improved therapeutic effect, probably owing to the enhanced tumor uptake by the active targeting and enhanced permeability retention (EPR) effect.

In addition, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of tumor slices confirmed that whole cells in control groups of tumors largely retained their normal morphology with distinctive nuclear structures and membrane. In the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX +671nm laser group, some inflammatory cell infiltrations, cell death, and errhysis with tissue structure damage was observed (Fig. 7E). The in vivo anticancer results strongly suggested that GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX under laser irradiation could provide remarkable chemophotothermal synergistic effect for cancer therapy. Meanwhile, H&E staining of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney showed that GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX under laser irradiation did not have any significant side effects to normal tissues of these mice.

In summary, we prepared a novel crosslinked GdN@CQDs micelles with lower cytotoxicity. The use of GdN@CQDs/GP is not only limited to optical monitoring but also applied to deliver drug to the cell nuclei. Additionally, the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX can maintain drug resistance in tumor cells for a long time, because GdN@CQDs with GP has reduced the rate of drug release. Their fluorescence and paramagnetic properties make them an ideal nano-platform for dual model imaging and chemotherapy. The cell toxicity test results indicate that the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX possesses good biocompatibility and anticancer effects. Compared with the GdN@CQDs/DOX (13.2%), the crosslinked GdN@CQDs has high drug loading capacity (31.2%) for DOX. These features make the GdN@CQDs/GP an excellent probe for bio-imaging and bio-labeling which has been well demonstrated by imaging of live A549 cells. The loaded DOX can be triggered by external NIR light and lower intracellular pH value. The in vitro and in vivo results demonstrate that the GdN@CQDs/GP-DOX has the ability to achieve synergistic therapeutic effect.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by the Jiangsu six category outstanding talent (2012-NY-031), Jiangsu province science and technology support plan (BE2014327, BE2015367), the Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund CX(15)1016, JHB05-21 and Nanjing-321, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.07.138.
